mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 2 Series 216i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned BMW 2 Series 216i (B38) engine typically costs £3,200–£5,000 supply only, depending on the generation and power output. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 2 Series 216i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B38 three-cylinder engine has been in production since 2013 and has a mixed reliability reputation. While there are no widespread reports of bottom-end failures, common issues include timing chain tensioner wear, oil consumption and timing cover leaks, cooling system component failures, and engine mount failures. Regular maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 2 Series 216i?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain tensioner wear (cold-start rattle is the early warning), oil consumption and timing cover oil leaks (burning smell after drives), engine mount failure (excessive vibration at idle), cooling system failures (thermostat and water pump), and turbocharger seal failure. All are manageable with preventative maintenance and early detection.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 2 Series 216i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a well-maintained 2 Series (values £10,000–£25,000+ depending on year and model), a £3,500–£4,500 reconditioned engine is 15–35% of the vehicle's value — often worth it. The BMW scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 2 Series 216i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code (B38A15A, B38A15, or B38A15P) is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 216i badge has appeared across multiple 2 Series body styles since 2014. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the timing chain, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain tensioner wear is repairable without replacing the engine — if caught early. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement costs £800–£1,800. If the chain snaps and valves meet pistons, a full engine replacement is usually required. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (B38 Family)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start lasting 1–3 seconds, engine warning light, loss of power, metallic noises from the front of the engine.
problems.replacementCost: £3,200–£5,000 (if chain skips and valves meet pistons) [PLACEHOLDER]

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The B38 timing chain tensioner is known to wear prematurely, a common issue across the B-series engine family. A failing tensioner can cause rattling noises and, if ignored, may lead to severe engine damage. Cold-start rattle is the early warning sign — ignore it and you risk piston-valve contact and a full engine replacement. Regular oil changes with the correct BMW-approved oil are the best prevention. If you hear any rattle on startup, have the timing system inspected immediately.

problems.title: Oil Consumption & Timing Cover Leaks
problems.symptoms: Low oil warning light, needing to top up oil frequently, burning oil smell after long drives, oil pooling on the engine bay liner under the timing cover, slow oil level drop over weeks.
problems.repairCost: £150–£300 (regular oil top-ups and monitoring) — £400–£900 (timing cover seal replacement) [PLACEHOLDER]
problems.replacementCost: £3,200–£5,000 (if oil starvation leads to engine damage) [PLACEHOLDER]

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; monitor oil levels weekly

problems.recommendation: Excessive oil consumption and timing cover oil leaks are frequently reported issues with the B38 engine. Owners have noted oil pooling on the bay liner and a burning smell after motorway runs. Check your oil level regularly — this engine is not one you can neglect. Use the correct grade of fully synthetic BMW-approved oil and consider reducing oil change intervals to 8,000 miles. If you notice a burning oil smell or oil on the front of the engine, have the timing cover seal inspected.

problems.title: Engine Mount Failure
problems.symptoms: Excessive cabin vibrations at idle, clunking noise on D-to-R shifts, mount visibly seeping fluid through cracked rubber sleeve, vibration through the floor.
problems.repairCost: £400–£800 (full set of engine and transmission mounts) [PLACEHOLDER]

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can cause additional wear on other components

problems.recommendation: BMW 216i owners have reported engine mount failures causing cabin vibrations and clunking noises. The front engine mount can develop cracked rubber and leak damping fluid. Some owners have needed a full set of engine and transmission mounts replaced. If you notice excessive vibration at idle or clunking on gear changes, have the mounts inspected — replacement is straightforward and restores smoothness.

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (Thermostat & Water Pump)
problems.symptoms: Running hot in traffic, temperature fluctuations, sweet smell from engine bay, no visible coolant leak.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if overheating occurs

problems.recommendation: Cooling system issues have been noted in B38 engines, particularly with the thermostat and water pump. Some owners have reported the engine running hot in traffic with no visible coolant leak. Overheating can lead to severe engine damage, so it's vital to address any cooling issues promptly. If you notice temperature fluctuations or the engine running hotter than normal, have the cooling system pressure-tested immediately.

problems.title: Turbocharger Failure
problems.symptoms: Check engine light, noticeable power loss under load, light blue smoke at hard throttle, whistling noise from the turbo area.
problems.replacementCost: £3,200–£5,000 (if turbo failure sends debris into the engine) [PLACEHOLDER]

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if power loss or turbo noise occurs

problems.recommendation: Some 216i owners have reported turbocharger seal failure, leading to power loss, blue smoke, and whistling noises. Turbo replacement is a specialist job — replacement is direct but may require ECU changes per BMW service bulletins. If you notice power loss under load or any unusual noises from the turbo area, have it inspected immediately. A £800–£2,000 turbo replacement is far cheaper than a full engine swap if debris enters the engine.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW 2 Series 216i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — far cheaper than catastrophic failure
Seal replacement is straightforward; catch it before oil starvation
Straightforward replacement; catch it before overheating
Replacement is viable if caught before debris enters engine
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This BMW 2 Series 216i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with oil changes at 8,000–10,000 mile intervals
* No cold-start rattle — timing chain tensioners should be healthy
* No oil leaks or burning oil smell — timing cover seal should be intact
* Cooling system in good order (no temperature fluctuations, no coolant loss)
* Engine mounts in good condition (no excessive vibration at idle)
avoidIf: * No proof of regular oil changes — this engine is unforgiving of neglect
* Cold-start rattle present — timing chain work is imminent
* Oil leaks or burning smell present — timing cover seal may be failing
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — the B38 can hide expensive faults
* Excessive vibration at idle — engine mounts may be failing

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW 2 Series 216i is the entry point into the premium compact segment, powered by a characterful 1.5-litre three-cylinder engine. The B38 is part of BMW's modular family and has been in production since 2013, but it demands respect: timing chain tensioner wear, oil consumption, cooling system failures, and engine mount issues are the key weak points. Buy on service history, maintain on a shortened schedule, and this is a genuinely engaging and frugal compact; neglect it, and you'll face expensive repairs.

cta.label: → Compare BMW 2 Series 216i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 2 Series 216i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle lasting more than 1–2 seconds (timing chain tensioner warning)
* Service History — Oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles are essential for the B38
* Oil Level — Check dipstick; excessive consumption indicates potential oil issues
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil on the front of the engine (timing cover seal) or burning smell after drives
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates cooling system issues
* Engine Temperature — Any fluctuations? Thermostat or water pump may be failing
* Idle Quality — Any excessive vibration? Engine mounts may be failing
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Timing, cooling, or turbo codes are most common
